name: accept-lifetime
detail: Set the time period during which the authentication key on a key chain is received as valid.
syntax: accept-lifetime [ local ] start-time { infinite end-time | duration seconds }

params.name: local
params.description: Specifies the time in local timezone.

params.name: start-time
params.description: Beginning time that the key specified by the key command is valid to be received. The syntax can be either of the following: hh : mm : ss month date year hh : mm : ss date month year • hh: Hours • mm: Minutes • ss: Seconds • month: First three letters of the month • date: Date (1-31) • year: Year (four digits) The default start time and the earliest acceptable date is January 1, 1993.

params.name: infinite
params.description: Key is valid to be received from the start-time value on.

params.name: end-time
params.description: Key is valid to be received from the start-time value until the end-timevalue. The syntax is the same as that for the start-timevalue. The end-time value must be after the start-timevalue. The default end time is an infinite time period. duration seconds Length of time (in seconds) that the key is valid to be received. The range is from 1 to 2147483646.

usageSummary: Only DRP Agent, Enhanced Interior Gateway Routing Protocol (EIGRP), and Routing Information Protocol ( RIP) Version 2 use key chains.  Specify a start-time value and one of the following values: infinite, end-time, or duration seconds.  We recommend running Network Time Protocol (NTP) or some other time synchronization method if you assign a lifetime to a key.

name: address-family ipv4
detail: Configure the Enhanced Interior Gateway Routing Protocol (EIGRP) for Multitopology Routing (MTR).
syntax: address-family ipv4 [unicast | multicast | vrf vrf-name] autonomous-system as-number
noForm: no address-family ipv4 [unicast | multicast | vrf vrf-name] autonomous-system as-number

params.name: unicast
params.description: (Optional) Specifies the unicast subaddress family.

params.name: multicast
params.description: (Optional) Specifies the multicast subaddress family.

params.name: vrf vrf-name
params.description: (Optional) Specifies the name of the virtual routing and forwarding (VRF). autonomous-system as-number Specifies the autonomous system number.

usageSummary: The address-family ipv4 command is used to enter router address family or subaddress family configuration mode to configure the exchange of address-family and subaddress-family prefixes.

name: address-family ipv6
detail: Enter the address family configuration mode for configuring routing sessions, such as Open Shortest Path First (OSPF), that uses the standard IPv6 address prefixes.

params.name: unicast
params.description: (Optional) Specifies the IPv6 unicast address prefixes.

params.name: vrf
params.description: (Optional) Specifies all the VPN routing and forwarding (VRF) instance tables or a specific VRF table for an IPv6 address.

params.name: vrf-name
params.description: (Optional) A specific VRF table for an IPv6 address.

usageSummary: The address-family ipv6 command places the router in address family configuration mode (prompt: config-router-af), from which you can configure routing sessions that use the standard IPv6 address prefixes.

name: address-family l2vpn
detail: Enter address family configuration mode to configure a routing session using Layer 2 Virtual Private Network (VPN) endpoint provisioning address information.

params.name: evpn
params.description: (Optional) Specifies L2VPN Ethernet Virtual Private Network (EVPN) endpoint provisioning address information.

params.name: vpls
params.description: (Optional) Specifies L2VPN Virtual Private LAN Service (VPLS) endpoint provisioning address information.

usageSummary: The address-family l2vpn command places the device in address family configuration mode (prompt: config-router-af), from which you can configure routing sessions that support Layer 2 VPN endpoint provisioning.  BGP support for the Layer 2 VPN address family introduces a BGP-based autodiscovery mechanism to distribute Layer 2 VPN endpoint provisioning information.  BGP uses a separate Layer 2 VPN routing information base (RIB) to store endpoint provisioning information, which is updated each time any Layer 2 virtual forwarding instance (VFI) is configured.

name: aggregate-address
detail: Create an aggregate entry in a Border Gateway Protocol (BGP) database.
syntax: aggregate-address address mask [as-set] [as-confed-set] [summary-only] [suppress-map map-name] [advertise-map map-name] [attribute-map map-name] map-name] [advertise-map map-name] [attribute-map map-name]

params.name: address
params.description: Aggregate address.

params.name: mask
params.description: Aggregate mask.

params.name: as-set
params.description: (Optional) Generates autonomous system set path information.

params.name: as-confed-set
params.description: (Optional) Generates autonomous confederation set path information.

params.name: summary-only
params.description: (Optional) Filters all more-specific routes from updates.

params.name: suppress-map map-name
params.description: (Optional) Specifies the name of the route map used to select the routes to be suppressed.

params.name: advertise-map map-name
params.description: (Optional) Specifies the name of the route map used to select the routes to create AS_SET origin communities.

params.name: attribute-map map-name
params.description: (Optional) Specifies the name of the route map used to set the attribute of the aggregate route.

usageSummary: You can implement aggregate routing in BGP and Multiprotocol BGP (mBGP) either by redistributing an aggregate route into BGP or mBGP, or by using the conditional aggregate routing feature.  Using the aggregate-addresscommand with no keywords will create an aggregate entry in the BGP or mBGP routing table if any more-specific BGP or mBGP routes are available that fall within the specified range.  (A longer prefix that matches the aggregate must exist in the Routing Information Base (RIB).

name: area nssa
detail: Configure a not-so-stubby area ( NSSA).

params.name: area-id
params.description: Identifier for the stub area or NSSA. The identifier can be specified as either a decimal value or an IP address.

params.name: no-redistribution
params.description: (Optional) Used when the router is an NSSA Area Border Router (ABR) and you want the redistribute command to import routes only into the normal areas, but not into the NSSA area.

params.name: default-information-
params.description: (Optional) Used to generate a Type 7 default into the NSSA area. This

params.name: originate
params.description: keyword takes effect only on the NSSA ABR or the NSSA Autonomous System Boundary Router (ASBR).

params.name: metric
params.description: (Optional) Specifies the OSPF default metric.

params.name: metric-type
params.description: (Optional) Specifies the OSPF metric type for default routes.

params.name: no-summary
params.description: (Optional) Allows an area to be an NSSA but not have summary routes injected into it.

params.name: nssa-only
params.description: (Optional) Limits the default advertisement to this NSSA area by setting the propagate (P) bit in the type-7 LSA to zero.

modes: Router address family topology configuration (config-router-af-topology) Router configuration (config-router)
usageSummary: To remove the specified area from the software configuration, use the no area area-idcommand (with no other keywords).  That is, the no area area-id command removes all area options, including area authentication, area default-cost, area nssa, area range, area stub, and area virtual-link.  Release 12.

name: area virtual-link
detail: Define an Open Shortest Path First (OSPF) virtual link.
syntax: area area-id virtual-link router-id authentication key-chain chain-name [hello-interval seconds] [retransmit-interval seconds] [transmit-delay seconds] [dead-interval seconds] [ttl-security hops hop-count] Table 145:
noForm: no area area-id virtual-link router-id authentication key-chain chain-name

params.name: area-id
params.description: Area ID assigned to the virtual link. This can be either a decimal value or a valid IPv6 prefix. There is no default.

params.name: router-id
params.description: Router ID associated with the virtual link neighbor. The router ID appears in the show ip ospf or show ipv6 display command. There is no default.

params.name: authentication
params.description: Enables virtual link authentication.

params.name: key-chain
params.description: Configures a key-chain for cryptographic authentication keys.

params.name: chain-name
params.description: Name of the authentication key that is valid.

params.name: hello-interval seconds
params.description: (Optional) Specifies the time (in seconds) between the hello packets that the Cisco IOS software sends on an interface. The hello interval is an unsigned integer value to be advertised in the hello packets. The value must be the same for all routers and access servers attached to a common network. The range is from 1 to 8192. The default is 10.

params.name: retransmit-interval seconds
params.description: (Optional) Specifies the time (in seconds) between link-state advertisement (LSA) retransmissions for adjacencies belonging to the interface. The retransmit interval is the expected round-trip delay between any two routers on the attached network. The value must be greater than the expected round-trip delay. The range is from 1 to 8192. The default is 5.

params.name: transmit-delay seconds
params.description: (Optional) Specifies the estimated time (in seconds) required to send a link-state update packet on the interface. The integer value that must be greater than zero. LSAs in the update packet have their age incremented by this amount before transmission. The range is from 1 to 8192. The default value is 1. Command Reference, Cisco IOS XE 17.15.x (Catalyst 9500 Switches) 1500 Routing area virtual-link

params.name: dead-interval seconds
params.description: (Optional) Specifies the time (in seconds) that hello packets are not seen before a neighbor declares the router down. The dead interval is an unsigned integer value. The default is four times the hello interval, or 40 seconds. As with the hello interval, this value must be the same for all routers and access servers attached to a common network.

params.name: ttl-security hops hop-count
params.description: (Optional) Configures Time-to-Live (TTL) security on a virtual link. The hop-count argument range is from 1 to 254.

usageSummary: In OSPF, all areas must be connected to a backbone area.  A lost connection to the backbone can be repaired by establishing a virtual link.  The shorter the hello interval, the faster topological changes will be detected, but more routing traffic will ensue.

name: auto-summary
detail: Configure automatic summarization of subnet routes into network-level routes.
usageSummary: BGP automatically summarizes routes to classful network boundaries when this command is enabled.  Route summarization is used to reduce the amount of routing information in routing tables.  Automatic summarization applies to connected, static, and redistributed routes.

name: authentication
detail: Configure authentication in a Bidirectional Forwarding Detection (BFD) template for single hop sessions.

params.name: authentication-type
params.description: Authentication type. Valid values are md5, meticulous-md5, meticulous-sha1, and sha-1. keychain keychain-name Configures an authentication key chain with the specified name. The maximum number of characters allowed in the name is 32.

usageSummary: You can configure authentication in single hop templates.  We recommend that you configure authentication to enhance security.  Authentication must be configured on each BFD source-destination pair, and authentication parameters must match on both devices.

name: bfd
detail: To set the baseline Bidirectional Forwarding Detection (BFD) session parameters on an interface, use the bfd interface configuration mode. To remove the baseline BFD session parameters, use the no form of this command
syntax: bfd interval milliseconds min_rx milliseconds multiplier multiplier-value
noForm: no bfd interval milliseconds min_rx milliseconds multiplier multiplier-value

params.name: interval milliseconds
params.description: Specifies the rate, in milliseconds, at which BFD control packets will be sent to BFD peers. The valid range for the milliseconds argument is from 50 to 9999.

params.name: min_rx milliseconds
params.description: Specifies the rate, in milliseconds, at which BFD control packets will be expected to be received from BFD peers. The valid range for the milliseconds argument is from 50 to 9999.

params.name: multiplier
params.description: Specifies the number of consecutive BFD control packets that must be missed

params.name: multiplier-value
params.description: from a BFD peer before BFD declares that the peer is unavailable and the Layer 3 BFD peer is informed of the failure. The valid range for the multiplier-valueargument is from 3 to 50.

usageSummary: The bfd command can be configured on SVI, Ethernet and port-channel interfaces.  If BFD runs on a port channel interface, BFD has a timer value restriction of 750 * 3 milliseconds.  The bfd interval configuration is not removed when: • an IPv4 address is removed from an interface • an IPv6 address is removed from an interface • IPv6 is disabled from an interface • an interface is shutdown • IPv4 CEF is disabled globally or locally on an interface • IPv6 CEF is disabled globally or locally on an interface The bfd interval configuration is removed when the subinterface on which its is configured is removed.

name: bfd all-interfaces
detail: Enable Bidirectional Forwarding Detection (BFD) for all interfaces participating in the routing process.
usageSummary: To enable BFD for all interfaces, enter the bfd all-interfaces command in router configuration mode

name: bfd check-ctrl-plane-failure
detail: Enable Bidirectional Forwarding Detection (BFD) control plane failure checking for the Intermediate System-to-Intermediate System (IS-IS) routing protocol.
usageSummary: The bfd check-ctrl-plane-failure command can be configured for an IS-IS routing process only.  The command is not supported on other protocols.  When a switch restarts, a false BFD session failure can occur, where neighboring routers behave as if a true forwarding failure has occurred.

name: bfd echo
detail: Enable Bidirectional Forwarding Detection (BFD) echo mode.
usageSummary: Echo mode is enabled by default.  Entering the no bfd echo command without any keywords turns off the sending of echo packets and signifies that the switch is unwilling to forward echo packets received from BFD neighbor switches.  When echo mode is enabled, the desired minimum echo transmit interval and required minimum transmit interval values are taken from the bfd interval milliseconds min_rx milliseconds parameters, respectively.

name: bfd slow-timers
detail: Configure the Bidirectional Forwarding Detection (BFD) slow timers value.

name: bfd template
detail: Create a Bidirectional Forwarding Detection (BFD) template and to enter BFD configuration mode.
usageSummary: Even if you have not created the template by using the bfd-template command, you can configure the name of the template under an interface, but the template is considered invalid until you define the template.  You do not have to reconfigure the template name again.  It becomes valid automatically.

name: bfd-template single-hop
detail: Bind a single hop Bidirectional Forwarding Detection (BFD) template to an interface.

params.name: single-hop
params.description: Creates the single-hop BFD template. template-name Template name.

usageSummary: The bfd-template command allows you to create a BFD template and places the device in BFD configuration mode.  The template can be used to specify a set of BFD interval values.  BFD interval values specified as part of the BFD template are not specific to a single interface.

name: bgp graceful-restart
detail: Enable the Border Gateway Protocol (BGP) graceful restart capability globally for all BGP neighbors.
syntax: bgp graceful-restart [extended | restart-time seconds | stalepath-time seconds] [all]

params.name: extended
params.description: (Optional) Enables BGP graceful restart extension.

params.name: restart-time seconds
params.description: (Optional) Sets the maximum time period that the local router will wait for a graceful-restart-capable neighbor to return to normal operation after a restart event occurs. The default value for this argument is 120 seconds. The configurable range of values is from 1 to 3600 seconds.

params.name: stalepath-time seconds
params.description: (Optional) Sets the maximum time period that the local router will hold stale paths for a restarting peer. All stale paths are deleted after this timer expires. The default value for this argument is 360 seconds. The configurable range of values is from 1 to 3600 seconds

params.name: all
params.description: (Optional) Enables BGP graceful restart capability for all address family modes.

usageSummary: The bgp graceful-restart command is used to enable or disable the graceful restart capability globally for all BGP neighbors in a BGP network.  The graceful restart capability is negotiated between nonstop forwarding (NSF)-capable and NSF-aware peers in OPEN messages during session establishment.  If the graceful restart Command Reference, Cisco IOS XE 17.

name: clear proximity ip bgp
detail: Reset Border Gateway Protocol (BGP) connections using hard or soft reconfiguration.
syntax: clear proximity ip bgp {* | allautonomous-system-numberneighbor-address | peer-group group-name} [in [prefix-filter] | out | slow | soft [in [prefix-filter] | out | slow]]

params.description: Specifies that all current BGP sessions will be reset.

params.name: all
params.description: (Optional) Specifies the reset of all address family sessions. autonomous-system-number Number of the autonomous system in which all BGP peer sessions will be reset. Number in the range from 1 to 65535. • In Cisco IOS Release 12.0(32)SY8, 12.0(33)S3, 12.2(33)SRE, 12.2(33)XNE, 12.2(33)SXI1, Cisco IOS XE Release 2.4, and later releases, 4-byte autonomous system numbers are supported in the range from 65536 to 4294967295 in asplain notation and in the range from 1.0 to 65535.65535 in asdot notation. • In Cisco IOS Release 12.0(32)S12, 12.4(24)T, and Cisco IOS XE Release 2.3, 4-byte autonomous system numbers are supported in the range from 1.0 to 65535.65535 in asdot notation only. For more details about autonomous system number formats, see the router bgp command.

params.name: neighbor-address
params.description: Specifies that only the identified BGP neighbor will be reset. The value for this argument can be an IPv4 or IPv6 address. peer-group group-name Specifies that only the identified BGP peer group will be reset.

params.name: in
params.description: (Optional) Initiates inbound reconfiguration. If neither the in nor out keywords are specified, both inbound and outbound sessions are reset.

params.name: prefix-filter
params.description: (Optional) Clears the existing outbound route filter (ORF) prefix list to trigger a new route refresh or soft reconfiguration, which updates the ORF prefix list.

params.name: out
params.description: (Optional) Initiates inbound or outbound reconfiguration. If neither the in nor out keywords are specified, both inbound and outbound sessions are reset.

params.name: slow
params.description: (Optional) Clears slow-peer status forcefully and moves it to original update group.

params.name: soft
params.description: (Optional) Initiates a soft reset. Does not tear down the session.

modes: Command Reference, Cisco IOS XE 17.15.x (Catalyst 9500 Switches) 1519 Routing clear proximity ip bgp
usageSummary: Theclearproximity ip bgp command can be used to initiate a hard reset or soft reconfiguration.  A hard reset tears down and rebuilds the specified peering sessions and rebuilds the BGP routing tables.  A soft reconfiguration uses stored prefix information to reconfigure and activate BGP routing tables without tearing down existing peering sessions.

name: default-information originate
detail: Generate a default external route into an Open Shortest Path First (OSPF) routing domain.
syntax: default-information originate [always] [metric metric-value] [metric-type type-value] [route-map map-name] map-name]

params.name: always
params.description: (Optional) Always advertises the default route regardless of whether the software has a default route. Note The always keyword includes the following exception when the route map is used. When a route map is used, the origination of the default route by OSPF is not bound to the existence of a default route in the routing table and the always keyword is ignored.

params.name: metric
params.description: metric-value (Optional) Metric used for generating the default route. If you omit a value and do not specify a value using the default-metric router configuration command, the default metric value is 10. The value used is specific to the protocol.

params.name: metric-type
params.description: (Optional) External link type associated with the default route that is advertised

params.name: type-value
params.description: into the OSPF routing domain. It can be one of the following values: • Type 1 external route. • Type 2 external route. The default is type 2 external route. route-map map-name (Optional) The routing process will generate the default route if the route map is satisfied.

modes: Router configuration (config-router) Router address family topology configuration (config-router-af-topology)
usageSummary: Whenever you use the redistribute or the default-information router configuration command to redistribute routes into an OSPF routing domain, the Cisco IOS software automatically becomes an Autonomous System Boundary Router (ASBR).  However, an ASBR does not, by default, generate a default route into the OSPF routing domain.  The software must still have a default route for itself before it generates one, except when you have specified the always keyword.

name: default-metric
detail: Set a default metric for routes redistributed into Border Gateway Protocol (BGP).

params.name: number
params.description: Default metric value applied to the redistributed route. The range of values for this argument is from 1 to 4294967295.

usageSummary: The default-metric command is used to set the metric value for routes redistributed into BGP and can be applied to any external BGP (eBGP) routes received and subsequently advertised internally to iBGP peers.  This value is the Multi Exit Discriminator (MED) that is evaluated by BGP during the best path selection process.  The MED is a non-transitive value that is processed only within the local autonomous system and adjacent autonomous systems.

name: distance
detail: Define an administrative distance.

params.name: weight
params.description: Administrative distance. Range is 10 to 255. Used alone, the weight argument specifies a default administrative distance that the software uses when no other specification exists for a routing information source. Routes with a distance of 255 are not installed in the routing table. The table in the “Usage Guidelines” section lists the default administrative distances.

params.name: ip-address
params.description: (Optional) IP address in four-part dotted-decimal notation.

params.name: wildcard-mask
params.description: (Optional) Wildcard mask in four-part, dotted-decimal format. A bit set to 1 in the wildcard-mask argument instructs the software to ignore the corresponding bit in the address value. access-list-name (Optional) Name of an IP access list to be applied to incoming routing updates.

usageSummary: To use this command, you must be in a user group associated with a task group that includes the appropriate task IDs.  If the user group assignment is preventing you from using a command contact your AAA administrator for assistance.  An administrative distance is an integer from 10 to 255.

name: eigrp log-neighbor-changes
detail: Enable the logging of changes in Enhanced Interior Gateway Routing Protocol (EIGRP) neighbor adjacencies.
modes: Router configuration (config-router) Address-family configuration (config-router-af) Service-family configuration (config-router-sf)
usageSummary: This command enables the logging of neighbor adjacency changes to monitor the stability of the routing system and to help detect problems.  Logging is enabled by default.  To disable the logging of neighbor adjacency changes, use the no form of this command.

name: eigrp log-neighbor-warnings
detail: Enable the logging of Enhanced Interior Gateway Routing Protocol (EIGRP) neighbor warning messages.

params.name: seconds
params.description: (Optional) The time interval (in seconds) between repeated neighbor warning messages. The range is from 1 to 65535. The default is 10.

modes: Router configuration (config-router) Address-family configuration (config-router-af) Service-family configuration (config-router-sf)
usageSummary: When neighbor warning messages occur, they are logged by default.  With this command, you can disable and enable neighbor warning messages, and you can configure the interval between repeated neighbor warning messages.  To enable the logging of warning messages for an EIGRP address family, use the eigrp log-neighbor-warnings command in address-family configuration mode.

name: fast-reroute keep-all-paths
detail: Create a list of all the candidate repair paths considered when a per-prefix loop-free alternate (LFA) Fast Reroute (FRR) route is computed.
usageSummary: You can use the fast-reroute keep-all-paths command to display all the candidate repair paths that are considered when an LFA FRR repair path is computed.  You can use this list to troubleshoot repair paths without having to enable debugs.  However, this greatly increases memory consumption, and should, therefore, be reserved for testing.

name: fast-reroute load-sharing disable
detail: Disable Fast Reroute (FRR) load sharing among Equal Cost Multipath (ECMP) loop-free alternates (LFAs) in an Enhanced Interior Gateway Routing Protocol (EIGRP) network.
usageSummary: Use this command to disable FRR load sharing among ECMP LFAs when FRR can be enabled on a single LFA by using tiebreaking rules.  These rules are used to select the best LFA (repair path) for a primary path in an EIGRP network when many candidate LFAs are available.  However, if a tie-breaking rule cannot be applied to select LFAs, use the no form of this command to restore the device to its default settings.

name: fast-reroute per-prefix
detail: Enable Fast Reroute (FRR) per prefix in an Enhanced Interior Gateway Routing Protocol (EIGRP) network.

params.name: all
params.description: Enables FRR for all the available prefixes in the EIGRP network.

params.name: route-map
params.description: Enables FRR for prefixes that are specified by a route map. route-map-name Name of the route map.

name: fast-reroute per-prefix enable
detail: Configure a per-prefix LFA FRR path that redirects traffic to an alternative next hop other than the primary neighbor.
noForm: no fast-reroute per-prefix enable [area area-id ]prefix-priority {high | low}

params.name: area
params.description: (Optional) Specifies an area in which to enable LFA FRR.

params.name: area-id
params.description: OSPF area ID expressed as a decimal value, or in IP address format. prefix-priority Specifies the priority of prefixes to be protected.

params.name: high
params.description: Sets the prefix priority to high.

params.name: low
params.description: Sets the prefix priority to low.

name: fast-reroute per-prefix tie-break
detail: Configure the tiebreaking policy in selecting in an LFA FRR repair path.
syntax: fast-reroute per-prefix tie-break {broadcast-interface-disjoint | downstream | interface-disjoint | linecard-disjoint | node-protecting | primary-path | secondary-path | srlg} [required] {index attribute-priority | lowest-metric index attribute-priority} linecard-disjoint | node-protecting | primary-path | secondary-path | srlg} [required] {index attribute-priority | lowest-metric index attribute-priority}
noForm: no fast-reroute per-prefix tie-break {broadcast-interface-disjoint | downstream | interface-disjoint |

params.name: downstream
params.description: Configures LFAs whose metric to the protected destination is lower than the metric of the protecting node to the destination.

params.name: interface-disjoint
params.description: Configures the interface protection attribute.

params.name: linecard-disjoint
params.description: Configures the linecard protection attribute.

params.name: node-protecting
params.description: Configures the node-protecting repair path attribute.

params.name: primary-path
params.description: Configures the equal-cost multipath attribute.

params.name: secondary-path
params.description: Configures the not-equal-cost multipath attribute.

params.name: srlg
params.description: Configures the shared risk link group (SRLG) attribute.

params.name: required
params.description: (Optional) Specifies that the tiebreaker is required.

params.name: index
params.description: Specifies the tiebreak attribute priority.

params.name: attribute-priority
params.description: The tiebreak attribute priority number. Valid values are from 1 to 255.

params.name: lowest-metric
params.description: Configures the lowest metric repair path attribute.

usageSummary: You must configure the router ospf command before you can configure the fast-reroute per-prefix tie-break command.  You can use the show ip ospf fast-reroute command to display the default, or the current, tiebreak configuration.  The tiebreaker policy is evaluated in the configured or the default order.

name: fast-reroute tie-break
detail: Enable EIGRP FRR to select a loop-free alternate (LFA) from among multiple candidate LFAs for a given primary path by configuring a tiebreaking attribute.
syntax: fast-reroute tie-break {interface-disjoint | linecard-disjoint | lowest-backup-path-metric | srlg-disjoint} priority-number default fast-reroute tie-break {interface-disjoint | linecard-disjoint | lowest-backup-path-metric | srlg-disjoint}
noForm: no fast-reroute tie-break {interface-disjoint | linecard-disjoint | lowest-backup-path-metric | srlg-disjoint}

params.name: interface-disjoint
params.description: Enables EIGRP FRR to choose an LFA that does not share the outgoing interface with the primary path. The default priority is 20.

params.name: linecard-disjoint
params.description: Enables EIGRP FRR to choose an LFA that does not share the line card with the primary path. The default priority is 40. lowest-backup-path-metric Enables EIGRP FRR to choose the LFA with the lowest metric to the protected destination. The default priority is 30.

params.name: srlg-disjoint
params.description: Enables EIGRP FRR to choose an LFA that does not share any Shared Risk Link Group (SRLG) with the primary path. The default priority is 10.

params.name: priority-number
params.description: Priority number assigned to the tiebreaking attribute. The range is from 1 to 255.

usageSummary: Use this command to configure tiebreaking rules when there are multiple LFAs for a given primary path.  EIGRP allows you to use four attributes to configure tiebreaking rules.  Each of the interface-disjoint, linecard-disjoint, lowest-backup-path-metric, and srlg-disjoint keywords specifies an attribute and allows you to configure a tiebreaking rule based on the attribute.

name: ip authentication key-chain eigrp
detail: Enable authentication of Enhanced Interior Gateway Routing Protocol (EIGRP) packets.
syntax: ip authentication key-chain eigrp as-number key-chain
noForm: no ip authentication key-chain eigrp as-number key-chain

params.name: as-number
params.description: Autonomous system number to which the authentication applies.

params.name: key-chain
params.description: Name of the authentication key chain.

name: ip authentication mode eigrp
detail: Specify the type of authentication used in Enhanced Interior Gateway Routing Protocol (EIGRP) packets.
syntax: ip authentication mode eigrp as-number md5
noForm: no ip authentication mode eigrp as-number md5

params.name: as-number
params.description: Autonomous system number.

params.name: md5
params.description: Keyed Message Digest 5 ( MD5) authentication.

usageSummary: Configure authentication to prevent unapproved sources from introducing unauthorized or false routing messages.  When authentication is configured, an MD5 keyed digest is added to each EIGRP packet in the specified autonomous system.

name: ip bandwidth-percent eigrp
detail: Configure the percentage of bandwidth that may be used by Enhanced Interior Gateway Routing Protocol (EIGRP) on an interface.
noForm: no ip bandwidth-percent eigrp as-number percent

params.name: as-number
params.description: Autonomous system number.

params.name: percent
params.description: Percent of bandwidth that EIGRP may use.

usageSummary: EIGRP will use up to 50 percent of the bandwidth of a link, as defined by the bandwidth interface configuration command.  This command may be used if some other fraction of the bandwidth is desired.

name: ip cef load-sharing algorithm
detail: Select a Cisco Express Forwarding load-balancing algorithm, use theip cef load-sharing algorithm command in global configuration mode. To return to the default universal load-balancing algorithm.

params.name: original
params.description: Sets the load-balancing algorithm to the original algorithm based on a source and destination hash.

params.name: universal
params.description: Sets the load-balancing algorithm to the universal algorithm that uses a source and destination and an ID hash.

params.name: id
params.description: (Optional) Fixed identifier.

usageSummary: The original Cisco Express Forwarding load-balancing algorithm produced distortions in load sharing across multiple devices because of the use of the same algorithm on every device.  When the load-balancing algorithm is set to universal mode, each device on the network can make a different load sharing decision for each source-destination address pair, and that resolves load-balancing distortions.

name: ip community-list
detail: Configure a BGP community list and to control which routes are permitted or denied based on their community values.
syntax: Standard Community Lists ip community-list {standard | standard list-name} {deny | permit} [community-number] [AA:NN] [internet] [local-as] [no-advertise] [no-export] [gshut] Expanded Community Lists ip community-list {expanded | expanded list-name} {deny | permit} regexp
noForm: no ip community-list {standard | standard list-name} no ip community-list {expanded | expanded list-name}

params.name: standard
params.description: Standard community list number from 1 to 99 to identify one or more permit or deny groups of communities.

params.name: standard list-name
params.description: Configures a named standard community list.

params.name: deny
params.description: Denies routes that match the specified community or communities.

params.name: permit
params.description: Permits routes that match the specified community or communities.

params.name: community-number
params.description: (Optional) 32-bit number from 1 to 4294967200. A single community can be entered or multiple communities can be entered, each separated by a space. Command Reference, Cisco IOS XE 17.15.x (Catalyst 9500 Switches) 1552 Routing ip community-list

params.name: AA :NN
params.description: (Optional) Autonomous system number and network number entered in the 4-byte new community format. This value is configured with two 2-byte numbers separated by a colon. A number from 1 to 65535 can be entered for each 2-byte number. A single community can be entered or multiple communities can be entered, each separated by a space.

params.name: internet
params.description: (Optional) Specifies the Internet community. Routes with this community are advertised to all peers (internal and external).

params.name: local-as
params.description: (Optional) Specifies the local-as community. Routes with community are advertised to only peers that are part of the local autonomous system or to only peers within a subautonomous system of a confederation. These routes are not advertised to external peers or to other subautonomous systems within a confederation.

params.name: no-advertise
params.description: (Optional) Specifies the no-advertise community. Routes with this community are not advertised to any peer (internal or external).

params.name: no-export
params.description: (Optional) Specifies the no-export community. Routes with this community are advertised to only peers in the same autonomous system or to only other subautonomous systems within a confederation. These routes are not advertised to external peers. Command Reference, Cisco IOS XE 17.15.x (Catalyst 9500 Switches) 1553 Routing ip community-list

params.name: gshut
params.description: (Optional) Specifies the Graceful Shutdown (GSHUT) community.

params.name: expanded
params.description: Expanded community list number from 100 to 500 to identify one or more permit or deny groups of communities.

params.name: expanded list-name
params.description: Configures a named expanded community list.

params.name: regexp
params.description: Regular expression that is used to specify a pattern to match against an input string. Note Regular expressions can be used only with expanded community lists.

usageSummary: The ip community-list command is used to filter BGP routes based on one or more community values.  BGP community values are configured as a 32-bit number (old format) or as a 4-byte number (new format).  The new community format is enabled when the ip bgp-community new-format command is entered in global configuration mode.

name: ip prefix-list
detail: Create a prefix list or to add a prefix-list entry.
syntax: ip prefix-list {list-name [seq number] {deny | permit} network/length [ge ge-length] [le le-length] | description description | sequence-number} le-length]] | description description | sequence-number}
noForm: no ip prefix-list {list-name [seq number] [ {deny | permit} network/length [ge ge-length] [le

params.name: list-name
params.description: Configures a name to identify the prefix list. Do not use the word “detail” or “summary” as a list name because they are keywords in the show ip prefix-list command.

params.name: seq
params.description: (Optional) Applies a sequence number to a prefix-list entry.

params.name: number
params.description: (Optional) Integer from 1 to 4294967294. If a sequence number is not entered when configuring this command, default sequence numbering is applied to the prefix list. The number 5 is applied to the first prefix entry, and subsequent unnumbered entries are incremented by 5.

params.name: deny
params.description: Denies access for a matching condition.

params.name: permit
params.description: Permits access for a matching condition.

params.name: network / length
params.description: Configures the network address and the length of the network mask in bits. The network number can be any valid IP address or prefix. The bit mask can be a number from 1 to 32.

params.name: ge
params.description: (Optional) Specifies the lesser value of a range (the “from” portion of the range description) by applying the ge-length argument to the range specified. Note The ge keyword represents the greater than or equal to operator.

params.name: ge-length
params.description: (Optional) Represents the minimum prefix length to be matched.

params.name: le
params.description: (Optional) Specifies the greater value of a range (the “to” portion of the range description) by applying the le-length argument to the range specified. Note The le keyword represents the less than or equal to operator.

params.name: le-length
params.description: (Optional) Represents the maximum prefix length to be matched.

params.name: description
params.description: (Optional) Configures a descriptive name for the prefix list.

params.name: description
params.description: (Optional) Descriptive name of the prefix list, from 1 to 80 characters in length.

params.name: sequence-number
params.description: (Optional) Enables or disables the use of sequence numbers for prefix lists.

usageSummary: Use the ip prefix-list command to configure IP prefix filtering.  Prefix lists are configured with permit or deny keywords to either permit or deny a prefix based on a matching condition.  An implicit deny is applied to traffic that does not match any prefix-list entry.

name: ip hello-interval eigrp
detail: Configure the hello interval for an Enhanced Interior Gateway Routing Protocol (EIGRP) process.

params.name: as-number
params.description: Autonomous system number.

params.name: seconds
params.description: Hello interval (in seconds). The range is from 1 to 65535.

usageSummary: The default of 60 seconds applies only to low-speed, NBMA media.  Low speed is considered to be a rate of T1 or slower, as specified with the bandwidth interface configuration command.

name: ip hold-time eigrp
detail: Configure the hold time for an Enhanced Interior Gateway Routing Protocol (EIGRP) process.
noForm: no ip hold-time eigrp as-number seconds

params.name: as-number
params.description: Autonomous system number.

params.name: seconds
params.description: Hold time (in seconds). The range is from 1 to 65535.

usageSummary: On very congested and large networks, the default hold time might not be sufficient time for all routers and access servers to receive hello packets from their neighbors.  In this case, you may want to increase the hold time.  We recommend that the hold time be at least three times the hello interval.

name: ip load-sharing
detail: Enable load balancing for Cisco Express Forwarding on an interface.

params.name: per-destination
params.description: Enables per-destination load balancing for Cisco Express Forwarding on the interface.

usageSummary: Per-destination load balancing allows the device to use multiple, equal-cost paths to achieve load sharing.  Packets for a given source-destination host pair are guaranteed to take the same path, even if multiple, equal-cost paths are available.  Traffic for different source-destination host pairs tends to take different paths.

name: ip network-broadcast
detail: To receive and accept the network-prefix-directed broadcast packets, configure the ip network-broadcast command at the interface of the device.
usageSummary: Configure the ip network-broadcast command at the ingress interface before configuring the ip directed-broadcast command at the egress interface.  This ensures that the network-prefix-directed broadcast packets are received and accepted.  The ip network-broadcast command is disabled by default.

name: ip next-hop-self eigrp
detail: Enable the Enhanced Interior Gateway Routing Protocol (EIGRP) to advertise routes with the local outbound interface address as the next hop.

params.name: as-number
params.description: Autonomous system number.

usageSummary: EIGRP, by default, sets the next-hop value to the local outbound interface address for routes that it is advertising, even when advertising those routes back out of the same interface on which they were learned.  To change this default, you must use the no ip next-hop-self eigrp interface configuration command to instruct EIGRP to use the received next-hop value when advertising these routes.  Following are some exceptions to this guideline: • If your topology does not require spoke-to-spoke dynamic tunnels, you need not configure the no ip next-hop-self eigrp command.

name: ip ospf database-filter all out
detail: Filter outgoing link-state advertisements (LSAs) to an Open Shortest Path First (OSPF) interface.
noForm: no ip ospf database-filter all out

params.name: disable
params.description: (Optional) Disables the filtering of outgoing LSAs to an OSPF interface; all outgoing LSAs are flooded to the interface. Note This keyword is available only in virtual network interface mode.

usageSummary: This command performs the same function that the neighbor database-filter command performs on a neighbor basis.  If the ip ospf database-filter all out command is enabled for a virtual network and you want to disable it, use the disable keyword in virtual network interface configuration mode.

name: ip ospf fast-reroute per-prefix
detail: Configure an interface as a protecting or a protected interface in a per-prefix LFA repair path.

params.name: disable
params.description: (Optional) Specifies that the interface is either protecting or protected.

usageSummary: If you know from the network topology that an interface cannot be used to reroute traffic, for example, if it goes to a customer site, you can use the ip ospf fast-reroute per-prefix command to disable it from being protecting interface.

name: ip ospf name-lookup
detail: Configure Open Shortest Path First (OSPF) to look up Domain Name System (DNS) names for use in all OSPF show EXEC command displays.
usageSummary: This command makes it easier to identify a router because the router is displayed by name rather than by its router ID or neighbor ID.

name: ip split-horizon eigrp
detail: Enable Enhanced Interior Gateway Routing Protocol (EIGRP) split horizon.

params.name: as-number
params.description: Autonomous system number.

usageSummary: Use the no ip split-horizon eigrp command to disable EIGRP split horizon in your configuration.

name: ip summary-address eigrp
detail: Configure address summarization for the Enhanced Interior Gateway Routing Protocol (EIGRP) on a specified interface.
syntax: ip summary-address eigrp as-number ip-address mask [admin-distance] [leak-map name]
noForm: no ip summary-address eigrp as-number ip-address mask

params.name: as-number
params.description: Autonomous system number.

params.name: ip-address
params.description: Summary IP address to apply to an interface.

params.name: mask
params.description: Subnet mask.

params.name: admin-distance
params.description: (Optional) Administrative distance. Range: 0 to 255. Note Starting with Cisco IOS XE Release 3.2S, the admin-distance argument was removed. Use the summary-metric command to configure the administrative distance.

params.name: leak-map name
params.description: (Optional) Specifies the route-map reference that is used to configure the route leaking through the summary.

usageSummary: The ip summary-address eigrp command is used to configure interface-level address summarization.  EIGRP summary routes are given an administrative-distance value of 5.  The administrative-distance metric is used to advertise a summary without installing it in the routing table.

name: ip route static bfd
detail: Specify static route bidirectional forwarding detection (BFD) neighbors.
syntax: ip route static bfd {interface-type interface-number ip-address | vrf vrf-name} [group group-name] [passive] [unassociate] [passive] [unassociate]
noForm: no ip route static bfd {interface-type interface-number ip-address | vrf vrf-name} [group group-name]

params.name: interface-type interface-number
params.description: Interface type and number.

params.name: ip-address
params.description: IP address of the gateway, in A.B.C.D format.

params.name: vrf vrf-name
params.description: Specifies Virtual Routing and Forwarding (VRF) instance and the destination vrf name.

params.name: group group-name
params.description: (Optional) Assigns a BFD group. The group-name is a character string of up to 32 characters specifying the BFD group name.

params.name: unassociate
params.description: (Optional) Unassociates the static route configured for a BFD.

usageSummary: Use the ip route static bfd command to specify static route BFD neighbors.  All static routes that have the same interface and gateway specified in the configuration share the same BFD session for reachability notification.  All static routes that specify the same values for the interface-type, interface-number, and ip-address arguments will automatically use BFD to determine gateway reachability and take advantage of fast failure detection.

name: ipv6 route static bfd
detail: Specify static route Bidirectional Forwarding Detection for IPv6 (BFDv6) neighbors.
syntax: ipv6 route static bfd [vrf vrf-name] interface-type interface-number ipv6-address [unassociated]

params.name: vrf vrf-name
params.description: (Optional) Name of the virtual routing and forwarding (VRF) instance by which static routes should be specified.

params.name: interface-type interface-number
params.description: Interface type and number.

params.name: ipv6-address
params.description: IPv6 address of the neighbor.

params.name: unassociated
params.description: (Optional) Moves a static BFD neighbor from associated mode to unassociated mode.

usageSummary: Use the ipv6 route static bfd command to specify static route neighbors.  All of the static routes that have the same interface and gateway specified in the configuration share the same BFDv6 session for reachability notification.  BFDv6 requires that BFDv6 sessions are initiated on both endpoint routers.

name: match tag
detail: Filter routes that match specific route tags.

params.name: tag-value
params.description: Route tag value, in plain decimals. The valid range is from 0 to 4294967295. tag-value-dotted-decimal Route tag value, in dotted decimals. The valid range is from 0.0.0.0 to 255.255.255.255.

usageSummary: Ellipses (... ) in the command syntax indicate that your command input can include multiple values for the tag-value and the tag-value-dotted-decimal arguments.

name: metric weights
detail: Tune the Enhanced Interior Gateway Routing Protocol (EIGRP) metric calculations.
syntax: Router Configuration metric weights tos k1 k2 k3 k4 k5 Address Family Configuration metric weights tos [k1 [k2 [k3 [k4 [k5 [k6]]]]]]
noForm: no metric weights no metric weights

params.name: tos
params.description: Type of service. This value must always be zero. k1 k2 k3 k4 k5 k6 (Optional) Constants that convert an EIGRP metric vector into a scalar quantity. Valid values are 0 to 255. Given below are the default values: • k1: 1 • k2: 0 • k3: 1 • k4: 0 • k5: 0 • k6: 0 Note In address family configuration mode, if the values are not specified, default values are configured. The k6 argument is supported only in address family configuration mode.

usageSummary: Use this command to alter the default behavior of EIGRP routing and metric computation and to allow the tuning of the EIGRP metric calculation for a particular type of service (ToS).  If k5 equals 0, the composite EIGRP metric is computed according to the following formula: metric = [k1 * bandwidth + (k2 * bandwidth)/(256 – load) + k3 * delay + K6 * extended metrics] Command Reference, Cisco IOS XE 17. 15.

name: neighbor advertisement-interval
detail: Set the minimum route advertisement interval (MRAI) between the sending of BGP routing updates.

params.name: ip-address
params.description: IP address of the neighbor.

params.name: peer-group-name
params.description: Name of a BGP peer group.

params.name: seconds
params.description: Time (in seconds) is specified by an integer ranging from 0 to 600.

usageSummary: When the MRAI is equal to 0 seconds, BGP routing updates are sent as soon as the BGP routing table changes.  If you specify a BGP peer group by using the peer-group-name argument, all the members of the peer group will inherit the characteristic configured with this command.

name: neighbor default-originate
detail: Allow a BGP speaker (the local router) to send the default route 0.0.0.0 to a neighbor for use as a default route.

params.name: ip-address
params.description: IP address of the neighbor.

params.name: peer-group-name
params.description: Name of a BGP peer group.

params.name: route-map
params.description: map-name (Optional) Name of the route map. The route map allows route 0.0.0.0 to be injected conditionally.

usageSummary: This command does not require the presence of 0. 0. 0.

name: neighbor description
detail: Associate a description with a neighbor.

params.name: ip-address
params.description: IP address of the neighbor.

params.name: peer-group-name
params.description: Name of an EIGRP peer group. This argument is not available in address-family configuration mode.

params.name: text
params.description: Text (up to 80 characters in length) that describes the neighbor.

name: neighbor ebgp-multihop
detail: Accept and attempt BGP connections to external peers residing on networks that are not directly connected.

params.name: ip-address
params.description: IP address of the BGP-speaking neighbor.

params.name: ipv6-address
params.description: IPv6 address of the BGP-speaking neighbor.

params.name: peer-group-name
params.description: Name of a BGP peer group.

params.name: ttl
params.description: (Optional) Time-to-live in the range from 1 to 255 hops.

usageSummary: This feature should be used only under the guidance of Cisco technical support staff.  If you specify a BGP peer group by using the peer-group-name argument, all the members of the peer group will inherit the characteristic configured with this command.  To prevent the creation of loops through oscillating routes, the multihop will not be established if the only route to the multihop peer is the default route (0.

name: neighbor maximum-prefix
detail: Control how many prefixes can be received from a neighbor.

params.name: ip-address
params.description: IP address of the neighbor.

params.name: peer-group-name
params.description: Name of a Border Gateway Protocol (BGP) peer group.

params.name: maximum
params.description: Maximum number of prefixes allowed from the specified neighbor. The number of prefixes that can be configured is limited only by the available system resources on a router.

params.name: threshold
params.description: (Optional) Integer specifying at what percentage of the maximum-prefix limit the router starts to generate a warning message. The range is from 1 to 100; the default is 75.

params.name: restart
params.description: (Optional) Configures the router that is running BGP to automatically reestablish a peering session that has been disabled because the maximum-prefix limit has been exceeded. The restart timer is configured with the restart-interval argument.

params.name: restart-interval
params.description: (Optional) Time interval (in minutes) that a peering session is reestablished. The range is from 1 to 65535 minutes.

params.name: warning-only
params.description: (optional) Allows the router to generate a sys-log message when the maximum-prefix limitis exceeded, instead of terminating the peering session.

usageSummary: The neighbor maximum-prefixcommand allows you to configure a maximum number of prefixes that a Border Gateway Protocol (BGP) routing process will accept from the specified peer.  This feature provides a mechanism (in addition to distribute lists, filter lists, and route maps) to control prefixes received from a peer.  When the number of received prefixes exceeds the maximum number configured, BGP disables the peering session (by default).

name: neighbor peer-group
detail: Configure a BGP neighbor to be a member of a peer group.

params.name: ip-address
params.description: IP address of the BGP neighbor that belongs to the peer group specified by the peer-group-name argument.

params.name: ipv6-address
params.description: IPv6 address of the BGP neighbor that belongs to the peer group specified by the peer-group-name argument.

params.name: peer-group-name
params.description: Name of the BGP peer group to which this neighbor belongs.

usageSummary: The neighbor at the IP address indicated inherits all the configured options of the peer group.

name: neighbor peer-group
detail: Create a BGP or multiprotocol BGP peer group.

params.name: peer-group-name
params.description: Name of the BGP peer group.

usageSummary: Often in a BGP or multiprotocol BGP speaker, many neighbors are configured with the same update policies (that is, same outbound route maps, distribute lists, filter lists, update source, and so on).  Neighbors with the same update policies can be grouped into peer groups to simplify configuration and make update calculation more efficient.

name: neighbor route-map
detail: Apply a route map to incoming or outgoing routes.

params.name: ip-address
params.description: IP address of the neighbor.

params.name: peer-group-name
params.description: Name of a BGP or multiprotocol BGP peer group.

params.name: ipv6-address
params.description: IPv6 address of the neighbor.
(Optional) IPv6 link-local address identifier. This keyword needs to be added whenever a link-local IPv6 address is used outside the context of its interface.

params.name: map-name
params.description: Name of a route map.

params.name: in
params.description: Applies route map to incoming routes.

params.name: out
params.description: Applies route map to outgoing routes.

usageSummary: When specified in address family configuration mode, this command applies a route map to that particular address family only.  When specified in router configuration mode, this command applies a route map to IPv4 or IPv6 unicast routes only.  If an outbound route map is specified, it is proper behavior to only advertise routes that match at least one section of the route map.

name: neighbor update-source
detail: Have the Cisco software allow Border Gateway Protocol (BGP) sessions to use any operational interface for TCP connections.
syntax: neighbor {ip-address | ipv6-address[%]peer-group-name}update-source interface-type interface-number neighbor {ip-address | ipv6-address[%]peer-group-name}update-source interface-type interface-number

params.name: ip-address
params.description: IPv4 address of the BGP-speaking neighbor.

params.name: ipv6-address
params.description: IPv6 address of the BGP-speaking neighbor.
(Optional) IPv6 link-local address identifier. This keyword needs to be added whenever a link-local IPv6 address is used outside the context of its interface.

params.name: peer-group-name
params.description: Name of a BGP peer group.

params.name: interface-type
params.description: Interface type.

params.name: interface-number
params.description: Interface number.

usageSummary: This command can work in conjunction with the loopback interface feature described in the “Interface Configuration Overview” chapter of the Cisco IOS Interface and Hardware Component Configuration Guide.  If you specify a BGP peer group by using the peer-group-name argument, all the members of the peer group will inherit the characteristic configured with this command.  The neighbor update-source command must be used to enable IPv6 link-local peering for internal or external BGP sessions.

name: network
detail: Specify the networks to be advertised by the Border Gateway Protocol (BGP) and multiprotocol BGP routing processes.

params.name: network-number
params.description: Network that BGP or multiprotocol BGP will advertise.

params.name: mask network-mask
params.description: (Optional) Network or subnetwork mask with mask address.

params.name: nsap-prefix
params.description: Network service access point (NSAP) prefix of the Connectionless Network Service (CLNS) network that BGP or multiprotocol BGP will advertise. This argument is used only under NSAP address family configuration mode.

params.name: route-map map-tag
params.description: (Optional) Identifier of a configured route map. The route map should be examined to filter the networks to be advertised. If not specified, all networks are advertised. If the keyword is specified, but no route map tags are listed, no networks will be advertised.

usageSummary: BGP and multiprotocol BGP networks can be learned from connected routes, from dynamic routing, and from static route sources.  The maximum number of network commands you can use is determined by the resources of the router, such as the configured NVRAM or RAM.

name: network
detail: Specify the network for an Enhanced Interior Gateway Routing Protocol (EIGRP) routing process.

params.name: ip-address
params.description: IP address of the directly connected network.

params.name: wildcard-mask
params.description: (Optional) EIGRP wildcard bits. Wildcard mask indicates a subnetwork, bitwise complement of the subnet mask.

usageSummary: When the network command is configured for an EIGRP routing process, the router matches one or more local interfaces.  The network command matches only local interfaces that are configured with addresses that are within the same subnet as the address that has been configured with the networkcommand.  The router then establishes neighbors through the matched interfaces.

name: nsf
detail: Enable Cisco nonstop forwarding (NSF) operations for the Enhanced Interior Gateway Routing Protocol (EIGRP).
usageSummary: The nsf command is used to enable or disable EIGRP NSF support on an NSF-capable router.  NSF is supported only on platforms that support High Availability.

name: offset-list
detail: Add an offset to incoming and outgoing metrics to routes learned via Enhanced Interior Gateway Routing Protocol (EIGRP).

params.name: access-list-number |
params.description: Standard access list number or name to be applied. Access list number 0

params.name: access-list-name
params.description: indicates all networks (networks, prefixes, or routes). If the offset value is 0, no action is taken.

params.name: in
params.description: Applies the access list to incoming metrics.

params.name: out
params.description: Applies the access list to outgoing metrics.

params.name: offset
params.description: Positive offset to be applied to metrics for networks matching the access list. If the offset is 0, no action is taken.

params.name: interface-type
params.description: (Optional) Interface type to which the offset list is applied.

params.name: interface-number
params.description: (Optional) Interface number to which the offset list is applied.

modes: Router configuration (config-router) Address family topology configuration (config-router-af-topology)
usageSummary: The offset value is added to the routing metric.  An offset list with an interface type and interface number is considered extended and takes precedence over an offset list that is not extended.  Therefore, if an entry passes the extended offset list and the normal offset list, the offset of the extended offset list is added to the metric.

name: redistribute
detail: Redistribute routes from one routing domain into another routing domain.
syntax: redistribute protocol [process-id] {level-1 | level-1-2 | level-2} [autonomous-system-number] [metric {metric-value | transparent}] [metric-type type-value] [match {internal | external 1 | external 2}] [tag tag-value] [route-map map-tag] [subnets] [nssa-only] [metric {metric-value | transparent}] [metric-type type-value] [match {internal | external 1 | external 2}] [tag tag-value] [route-map map-tag] [subnets] [nssa-only]

params.name: protocol
params.description: Source protocol from which routes are being redistributed. It can be one of the following keywords: application, bgp, connected, eigrp, isis, mobile, ospf, rip, or static [ip]. The static [ip] keyword is used to redistribute IP static routes. The optional ip keyword is used when redistributing into the Intermediate System-to-Intermediate System (IS-IS) protocol. The application keyword is used to redistribute an application from one routing domain to another. You can redistribute more than one application to different routing protocols such as IS-IS, OSPF, Border Gateway Protocol (BGP), Enhanced Interior Gateway Routing Protocol (EIGRP) and Routing Information Protocol (RIP). The connected keyword refers to routes that are established automatically by virtue of having enabled IP on an interface. For routing protocols such as Open Shortest Path First (OSPF) and IS-IS, these routes will be redistributed as external to the autonomous system. Command Reference, Cisco IOS XE 17.15.x (Catalyst 9500 Switches) 1605 Routing redistribute (IP)

params.name: process-id
params.description: (Optional) For the application keyword, this is the name of an application. For the bgp or eigrp keyword, this is an autonomous system number, which is a 16-bit decimal number. For the isis keyword, this is an optional tag value that defines a meaningful name for a routing process. Creating a name for a routing process means that you use names when configuring routing. You can configure a router in two routing domains and redistribute routing information between these two domains. For the ospf keyword, this is an appropriate OSPF process ID from which routes are to be redistributed. This identifies the routing process. This value takes the form of a nonzero decimal number. For the rip keyword, no process-id value is needed. For the application keyword, this is the name of an application. By default, no process ID is defined.

params.name: level-1
params.description: Specifies that, for IS-IS, Level 1 routes are redistributed into other IP routing protocols independently.

params.name: level-1-2
params.description: Specifies that, for IS-IS, both Level 1 and Level 2 routes are redistributed into other IP routing protocols.

params.name: level-2
params.description: Specifies that, for IS-IS, Level 2 routes are redistributed into other IP routing protocols independently.

params.name: autonomous-system-number
params.description: (Optional) Autonomous system number for the redistributed route. The range is from 1 to 65535. • 4-byte autonomous system numbers are supported in the range from 1.0 to 65535.65535 in asdot notation only. For more details about autonomous system number formats, see the router bgp command.

params.name: metric metric-value
params.description: (Optional) When redistributing from one OSPF process to another OSPF process on the same router, the metric will be carried through from one process to the other if no metric value is specified. When redistributing other processes to an OSPF process, the default metric is 20 when no metric value is specified. The default value is 0.

params.name: metric transparent
params.description: (Optional) Causes RIP to use the routing table metric for redistributed routes as the RIP metric. Command Reference, Cisco IOS XE 17.15.x (Catalyst 9500 Switches) 1606 Routing redistribute (IP)

params.name: metric-type type value
params.description: (Optional) For OSPF, specifies the external link type associated with the default route advertised into the OSPF routing domain. It can be one of two values: • 1—Type 1 external route • 2—Type 2 external route If a metric-type is not specified, the Cisco IOS software adopts a Type 2 external route. For IS-IS, it can be one of two values: • internal—IS-IS metric that is < 63. • external—IS-IS metric that is > 64 < 128. The default is internal. match {internal | external1 | external2} (Optional) Specifies the criteria by which OSPF routes are redistributed into other routing domains. It can be one of the following: • internal—Routes that are internal to a specific autonomous system. • external 1—Routes that are external to the autonomous system, but are imported into OSPF as Type 1 external routes. • external 2—Routes that are external to the autonomous system, but are imported into OSPF as Type 2 external routes. The default is internal.

params.name: tag tag-value
params.description: (Optional) Specifies the 32-bit decimal value attached to each external route. This is not used by OSPF itself. It may be used to communicate information between Autonomous System Boundary Routers (ASBRs). If none is specified, the remote autonomous system number is used for routes from BGP and Exterior Gateway Protocol (EGP); for other protocols, zero (0) is used.

params.name: route-map
params.description: (Optional) Specifies the route map that should be interrogated to filter the importation of routes from this source routing protocol to the current routing protocol. If not specified, all routes are redistributed. If this keyword is specified, but no route map tags are listed, no routes will be imported.

params.name: map-tag
params.description: (Optional) Identifier of a configured route map. Command Reference, Cisco IOS XE 17.15.x (Catalyst 9500 Switches) 1607 Routing redistribute (IP)

params.name: subnets
params.description: (Optional) For redistributing routes into OSPF. Note Irrespective of whether the subnets keyword is configured or not, the subnets functionality is enabled by default. This automatic addition results in the redistribution of classless OSPF routes.

params.name: nssa-only
params.description: (Optional) Sets the nssa-only attribute for all routes redistributed into OSPF.

modes: Router configuration (config-router) Address family configuration (config-af) Address family topology configuration (config-router-af-topology)

name: redistribute
detail: Redistribute IPv6 routes from one routing domain into another routing domain.
syntax: redistribute protocol [process-id ][include-connected {level-1 | level-1-2 | level-2}][as-number][metric metric-value] {metric-type type-value}[nssa-only][tag tag-value][route-map map-tag] metric-value] {metric-type type-value}[nssa-only][tag tag-value][route-map map-tag]

params.name: protocol
params.description: Source protocol from which routes are redistributed. It can be one of the following keywords: bgp, connected, eigrp, isis, lisp, nd, omp, ospf (ospfv3), rip, or static.

params.name: process-id
params.description: (Optional) For the bgp or eigrp keyword, the process ID is an autonomous system number, which is a 16-bit decimal number. For the isis keyword, the process ID is an optional value that defines a meaningful name for a routing process. You can specify only one Intermediate System-to-Intermediate System (IS-IS) process per router. Creating a name for a routing process means that you use names when configuring routing. For the ospf keyword, the process ID is the number that is assigned administratively when the Open Shortest Path First (OSPF) for the IPv6 routing process is enabled. For the rip keyword, the process ID is an optional value that defines a meaningful name for an IPv6 Routing Information Protocol (RIP) routing process. include-connected (Optional) Allows the target protocol to redistribute routes that are learned by the source protocol and connected prefixes on those interfaces over which the source protocol is running.

params.name: level-1
params.description: Specifies that for IS-IS, Level 1 routes are redistributed into other IPv6 routing protocols independently.

params.name: level-1-2
params.description: Specifies that for IS-IS, both Level 1 and Level 2 routes are redistributed into other IPv6 routing protocols.

params.name: level-2
params.description: Specifies that for IS-IS, Level 2 routes are redistributed into other IPv6 routing protocols independently.

params.name: as-number
params.description: (Optional) Autonomous system number for the redistributed route.

params.name: metric
params.description: (Optional) When redistributing from one OSPF process to another OSPF process on

params.name: metric-value
params.description: the same router, the metric is carried through from one process to the other if no metric value is specified. When redistributing other processes to an OSPF process, the default metric is 20 when no metric value is specified. Command Reference, Cisco IOS XE 17.15.x (Catalyst 9500 Switches) 1613 Routing redistribute (IPv6)

params.name: metric-type
params.description: (Optional) Specifies the external link type that is associated with the default route that

params.name: type-value
params.description: is advertised into the routing domain. It can be one of two values: • 1: Type 1 external route • 2: Type 2 external route If no value is specified for the metric-type keyword, the Cisco IOS software adopts a Type 2 external route.

params.name: nssa-only
params.description: (Optional) Limits redistributed routes to not-so-stubby area (NSSA)

params.name: tag tag-value
params.description: (Optional) Specifies the 32-bit decimal value that is attached to each external route. This is not used by OSPF itself. It might be used to communicate information between Autonomous System Boundary Routers (ASBRs). If none is specified, then the remote autonomous system number is used for routes from the BGP and the Exterior Gateway Protocol (EGP); for other protocols, zero (0) is used.

params.name: route-map
params.description: (Optional) Specifies the route map that is checked to filter the import of routes from this source routing protocol to the current routing protocol. If the route-map keyword is not specified, all the routes are redistributed. If this keyword is specified, but no route map tags are listed, no routes are imported.

params.name: map-tag
params.description: (Optional) Identifier of a configured route map.

usageSummary: Changing or disabling a keyword does not affect the state of other keywords.  IS-IS ignores configured redistribution of routes, if any that are configured with the include-connected keyword.  IS-IS advertises a prefix on an interface if either IS-IS is running over the interface or the interface is configured as passive.

name: redistribute maximum-prefix
detail: Limit the number of prefixes that are redistributed into Open Shortest Path First (OSPF) or to generate a warning when the number of prefixes that are redistributed into OSPF reaches a maximum.

params.name: maximum
params.description: Integer from 1 to 4294967295 that specifies the maximum number of IP or IPv6 prefixes that can be redistributed into OSPF. When the warning-only keyword is configured, the maximum value specifies the number of prefixes that can be redistributed into OSPF before the system logs a warning message. Redistribution is not limited. The maximum number of IP or IPv6 prefixes that are allowed to be redistributed into OSPF, or the number of prefixes that are allowed to be redistributed into OSPF before the system logs a warning message, depends on whether the warning-only keyword is present. There is no default value for the maximum argument. If the warning-only keyword is also configured, this value does not limit redistribution; it is simply the number of redistributed prefixes that, when reached, causes a warning message to be logged.

params.name: percentage
params.description: (Optional) Integer from 1 to 100 that specifies the threshold value, as a percentage, at which a warning message is generated. The default percentage is 75.

params.name: warning-only
params.description: (Optional) Causes a warning message to be logged when the number of prefixes that are defined by the maximum argument has been exceeded. Additional redistribution is not prevented.

usageSummary: A network can be severely flooded if many IP or IPv6 prefixes are injected into the OSPF, perhaps by redistributing Border Gateway Protocol (BGP) into OSPF.  Limiting the number of redistributed prefixes prevents this potential problem.  When the redistribute maximum-prefix command is configured and the number of redistributed prefixes reaches the maximum value that is configured, no more prefixes are redistributed (unless the warning-only keyword is configured).

name: rewrite-evpn-rt-asn
detail: Enable the rewrite of the autonomous system number (ASN) portion of the EVPN route target extended community with the ASN of the target eBGP EVPN peer.
usageSummary: The rewrite-evpn-rt-asn command is required for the route target auto feature to be used to configure EVPN route targets.  Route target auto feature is implemented on all border leaf switches that support BGP EVPN.  The rewrite-evpn-rt-asn command only affects the following: • EVPN address family.

name: route-map
detail: Define conditions for redistributing routes from one routing protocol to another routing protocol, or to enable policy routing.
noForm: no route-map map-tag [permit | deny] [sequence-number] ordering-seq sequence-name

params.name: map-tag
params.description: Name for the route map.

params.name: permit
params.description: (Optional) Permits only the routes matching the route map to be forwarded or redistributed.

params.name: deny
params.description: (Optional) Blocks routes matching the route map from being forwarded or redistributed.

params.name: sequence-number
params.description: (Optional) Number that indicates the position a new route map will have in the list of route maps already configured with the same name. ordering-seq sequence-name (Optional) Orders the route maps based on the string provided.

usageSummary: Use the route-map command to enter route-map configuration mode.  Use route maps to redistribute routes, or to subject packets to policy routing.  Both these purposes are described here.

name: router-id
detail: Use a fixed router ID.

params.name: ip-address
params.description: Router ID in IP address format.

usageSummary: You can configure an arbitrary value in the IP address format for each router.  However, each router ID must be unique.  If this command is used on an OSPF router process which is already active (has neighbors), the new router-ID is used at the next reload or at a manual OSPF process restart.

name: router bgp
detail: Configure the Border Gateway Protocol (BGP) routing process.

params.name: autonomous-system-number
params.description: Number of an autonomous system that identifies the router to other BGP routers and tags the routing information that is passed along. Number in the range from 1 to 65535.

usageSummary: This command allows you to set up a distributed routing core that automatically guarantees the loop-free exchange of routing information between autonomous systems.  Cisco has implemented the following two methods of representing autonomous system numbers: • Asplain—Decimal value notation where both 2-byte and 4-byte autonomous system numbers are represented by their decimal value.  For example, 65526 is a 2-byte autonomous system number and 234567 is a 4-byte autonomous system number.

name: router eigrp
detail: Configure the EIGRP routing process.

params.name: autonomous-system-number
params.description: Autonomous system number that identifies the EIGRP services to the other EIGRP address-family routers. It is also used to tag routing information. Valid range is from 1 to 65535.

params.name: virtual-instance-name
params.description: EIGRP virtual instance name. This name must be unique among all the address-family router processes on a single router, but need not be unique among routers.

usageSummary: Configuring the router eigrp command with the autonomous-system-number argument creates an EIGRP configuration referred to as autonomous system (AS) configuration.  An EIGRP AS configuration creates an EIGRP routing instance that can be used for tagging routing information.  Configuring the router eigrp command with the virtual-instance-name argument creates an EIGRP configuration referred to as EIGRP named configuration.

name: router ospf
detail: Configure an OSPF routing process.

params.name: process-id
